ops.title: cc-sudo v0.2.0 E2E acceptance (hand-off — needs your Touch ID taps)
ops.body:
# cc-sudo E2E acceptance — hand-off runbook

The `[AUTO]` layer already passed: `swift build && swift test` in the cc-sudo
worktree is **green (89 tests)** — the tamper/replay/argv-swap/missing-key checks
(`VerifierTests`, `InstallerTests`, `MCPServerTests`) and `doctor` dry-run all run
with every root/launchctl/exec/socket boundary faked, so no root and no tap were
needed. What remains needs what a delegated agent can't supply: an **admin
password**, **live Touch ID taps**, and (optionally) a **second Mac**. Run these
yourself and record each observed result.

**Install from the RELEASED casks, never a local dev build** — cc-sudo's verifier
pins its own designated requirement and version; a dev build trips the version-skew
guard (`doctor` → exit **106**) by design.

## Exit-code reference (route on these)
| code | meaning |
|---|---|
| 0   | approved — command ran |
| 103 | denied — you declined the sheet; nothing ran |
| 104 | unavailable — no auth mechanism (the only degradable case) |
| 105 | verification failed — attestation/DR pin rejected |
| 106 | version skew — verifier vs client mismatch |

## [TAP] single-Mac acceptance

1. **Install both casks.**
   ```sh
   brew install --cask yasyf/tap/authkit
   brew install yasyf/tap/cc-sudo
   ```
2. **Install the root side** (one admin password; enrolls the Secure-Enclave key —
   expect one Touch ID during keygen).
   ```sh
   sudo cc-sudo install
   ```
3. **Trust chain, non-interactive** — expect every check green.
   ```sh
   cc-sudo doctor
   ```
4. **Probe the prompt path** (root + a tap). Run it **once in Terminal.app on the
   console** and **once over `ssh localhost` from a different login**. This is the
   productionized form of the spike RUNBOOK Steps 2–3 and settles the one open
   design question: whether `launchctl asuser` renders the sheet from an
   SSH-descended child, or whether synckitd's Aqua agent is mandatory for local
   non-GUI prompts. **Record the Terminal vs SSH outcomes separately — they can
   differ.**
   ```sh
   sudo cc-sudo doctor --probe-prompt
   ```
   - Terminal sheet appears + tap → pass → cc-sudo-exec can prompt locally via
     `asuser`, no synckitd needed for the local case.
   - SSH: sheet appears **on the physical console** (ssh shows nothing until you tap
     there) → pass → same-Mac SSH prompts via `asuser`. No sheet → synckitd's Aqua
     agent is the required path for local non-GUI prompts.
5. **Real privileged run — approve and deny.**
   ```sh
   cc-sudo run -- dscacheutil -flushcache      # tap → flushes, exit 0, stdout passed through
   cc-sudo run -- dscacheutil -flushcache      # cancel the sheet → exit 103, nothing runs
   ```
6. **MCP round-trip.** Start `cc-sudo mcp`, drive one `run_command` tool call over
   stdio, tap to approve → the reply is `{stdout, stderr, exit_code, verdict}`.
7. **Live tamper check.** Replace the installed `authkit` with an ad-hoc-signed stub
   of the same name and re-run `cc-sudo run -- …`: the verifier must reject it on the
   **designated-requirement pin before any sheet appears** — exit **105**, no tap
   possible. Restore the real cask afterward (`brew reinstall --cask yasyf/tap/authkit`).

## [2-MACHINE] optional — routed-peer approval + negative

Needs two Macs both on synckit ≥ 0.16.

1. `cc-sudo trust <peer>` in **both** directions (each enrolls the other's key).
2. **Lock** the origin Mac's screen.
3. From an ssh session into the locked origin, `cc-sudo run -- <cmd>` → the prompt
   **routes to the live peer**; tap **on the peer** → the command runs on the origin,
   exit 0.
4. **Negative:** remove the peer's enrolled key (`cc-sudo uninstall` on the peer, or
   delete the trust entry) and repeat → the routed verify must **hard-fail (105),
   never degrade to an unsigned verdict**. This proves routed approvals verify
   against enrolled peer keys and never fall back to unsigned.

## Teardown
`sudo cc-sudo uninstall` removes the verifier, sudoers rule, and trust store.
ops.when: running the cc-sudo end-to-end acceptance test on real hardware (admin password + live Touch ID; optional second Mac)
